items.description: Numeral '53.' inscribed in the upper right corner above the text block.
items.scholarly_note: Post-medieval foliation or pagination mark added during later cataloguing, rebinding, or Danish/Icelandic curation phases (likely 17th–19th c.). Not original to the c. 1270 production.

items.description: Large capital 'C' opening a textual unit, with a small circular dot or oculus immediately to its upper left.
items.scholarly_note: Semi-decorated initial marking a new section or stanza within Sigrdrífumál. The adjacent dot may be ornamental, a layout guide, or a minor separate mark; such letters commonly project into the margin for visual hierarchy.

items.description: Small stylized ship or longboat doodle composed of multiple parallel curved lines forming a pointed hull with several strakes or oar-like strokes and a raised end.
items.scholarly_note: Typical medieval pen doodle. Ship motifs recur in Norse manuscripts, reflecting seafaring culture or serving as idle pen practice. Executed in iron-gall ink consistent with the main text or a similar pen.

items.description: Large flourished calligraphic 'S' with extensions, accompanied by a small cross or 'x', dots, and the adjacent letter sequence 'Speer.'
items.scholarly_note: Pen trials testing quill nib, ink flow, and stroke control, commonly placed in margins after finishing a page. The 'S' permits sweeping curves; 'Speer.' may represent a word fragment, name practice, or nonsense sequence.

items.description: Irregular dark brown patches and spots concentrated in the lower left margin and along the bottom edge, including at least two distinct areas.
items.scholarly_note: Age-related discoloration, foxing, or localized water/liquid staining combined with natural vellum degradation and possible iron-gall ink interaction. Common on exposed margins of 13th-century Icelandic calfskin manuscripts.

items.description: Small solid black diamond- or lozenge-shaped mark near the right edge of the lower margin.
items.scholarly_note: Interpretation uncertain; geometric regularity suggests possible deliberate marker (reference, quire, or ownership symbol), ink drop, or later annotation rather than purely accidental damage.

items.description: Faint linear abrasions and surface scratches visible across the lower margin, especially near stained areas and the vellum grain.
items.scholarly_note: Cumulative handling wear and/or preparation marks from the parchmenter's scraping knife. The hair side of vellum is more prone to such surface texture and abrasion.

scratches: faint linear abrasions in lower margin near stains and boat doodle
general hair-side surface wear and follicle patterns across vellum
damage_notes: brown staining patches in lower left margin and bottom edge
irregular, worn bottom edge of the leaf with minor nicks
small geometric black mark in lower right margin
typical edge wear and minor cockling consistent with 750+ years of use
summary: Page 62 (marked 53.) preserves a section of Sigrdrífumál in a professional Gothic book hand on well-prepared calf vellum. The text block is clean and uniform with no visible interlinear corrections, erasures, or scribal interventions. Marginal activity is confined to the lower margin, featuring a ship doodle, pen trials with a flourished 'S' and 'Speer.', brown age stains, a small diamond mark, and faint scratches. A later '53.' foliation appears in the upper right. No faces, animals, holes, or offset ink are evident. The surface condition is typical for a well-used 13th-century Icelandic manuscript.
